entities.description: A 2006 uncorrected OCR encoding at TEI in Libraries Level 1. It retains the 1871 title page and poem, supplies placeholders for omitted front and back matter, and links 148 TIFF page images through PB scaffolding.
entities.label: University of Michigan CME item AHA2700.0001.001
notes: The 709 L elements are encoded editorial positions matching Skeat's lineation; they are not 709 separately verified physical lines in the manuscript.

The XML verbal content outside the poem is limited to the title page. Its two omitted-front-matter DIV1 placeholders, omitted-back-matter DIV1 placeholder, and 148 PB elements represent carrier structure and linked images, not extra intellectual sections of Joseph.

bibliographic.citation: Walter W. Skeat, ed., Joseph of Arimathie: otherwise called The Romance of the Seint Graal, or Holy Grail, Early English Text Society, Original Series 44 (London: published for the Early English Text Society by N. Trübner & Co., 1871).
bibliographic.publisher: N. Trübner & Co. for the Early English Text Society
bibliographic.series: Early English Text Society, Original Series 44
bibliographic.title: Joseph of Arimathie: otherwise called The Romance of the Seint Graal, or Holy Grail

entities.description: The immediate scholarly edition behind the U-M encoding. Skeat printed the poem on pp. 1–23 after carefully revising George Parker's transcript; he supplied lineation, punctuation, hyphenation, and expanded contractions while claiming the text was otherwise exact.
entities.label: Walter W. Skeat's 1871 edition of Joseph of Arimathie
notes: The containing volume has four Joseph pieces. Only the first poem on printed pp. 1–23 is the target work.
The separately bound Legends of the Holy Rood and the prose and verse appendix pieces are outside this work's source boundary.
Skeat's c. 1350 composition date is retained as history of scholarship; the work metadata follows Carley's modern c. 1375 synthesis.

rights.basis: The edition was published in 1871. The Internet Archive item applies Public Domain Mark 1.0 to the scanned object; Skeat died in 1912.
rights.component: Skeat's 1871 edition and editorial matter
rights.jurisdiction: United States
notes: The Public Domain Mark is a status label, not a license.

access.accessMethod: Search Bodleian and Skeat archival catalogs or enquire with the holding institutions.
access.entityLabel: George Parker's working transcript of the Vernon Joseph
notes: The negative finding is date-bounded and does not establish that the transcript is lost.

entities.description: The transcript that Bodleian assistant George Parker prepared from the Vernon witness for Skeat. Skeat calls it excellent and says the printed copy was made after his own careful revision.
entities.label: George Parker's working transcript of the Vernon Joseph
notes: No public catalog record or surviving copy was identified in this research. The entity records Skeat's expressly stated workflow, not a claim that the transcript is extant.

access.accessMethod: Consult Bodleian Libraries reader and special-collections procedures; use the public surrogate when sufficient.
access.entityLabel: Bodleian Library MS. Eng. poet. a. 1, ff. 403ra–404vb
notes: No onsite consultation was undertaken for this record.

entities.description: The one witness recorded for DIMEV 4864 in release 2.0-alpha.1. Joseph begins defectively after missing f. 402 and occupies ff. 403ra–404vb. It ends in f. 404vb before the following Life of Pilate and Judas begins in that same folio and column.

holding.notes: The current catalog's item 363 gives Joseph at ff. 403ra–404vb; item 364 begins the following Pilate/Judas text at f. 404vb.

entities.label: Bodleian Library MS. Eng. poet. a. 1, ff. 403ra–404vb
notes: One-witness language is scoped to DIMEV 2.0-alpha.1, not asserted as an unchanging universal census.
The work is acephalous because f. 402 is lost; Skeat nevertheless found no sign of loss at the end.

physicalDescription.extent: Modern foliation: 342 leaves plus preliminary and final leaves; incomplete, with 71 leaves thought lost from an original 413. Joseph occupies ff. 403ra–404vb in the manuscript's own current item sequence, with f. 402 missing.
physicalDescription.format: Large Middle English religious and didactic miscellany, normally in gatherings of eight and written in two or three columns
notes: The codex is English and associated with the West Midlands; DIMEV's manuscript data records eLALME linguistic profile 7630, Worcestershire.
The manuscript presents Joseph continuously like prose; capitals, paraphs, and punctus-like marks informed Skeat's editorial lineation.

workPortion.label: Joseph of Arimathie, and only Joseph within the shared final surface

dateStatements.notes: Skeat's older estimate was c. 1350 and no later than 1360.

entities.description: The conceptual Middle English work, distinct from its single currently recorded physical witness and later editorial and digital representations. The surviving text is acephalous but apparently complete at the end.
entities.label: Anonymous Middle English Joseph of Arimathie

rights.basis: The anonymous work was composed in the fourteenth century and is far outside any subsisting copyright term; this does not determine rights in modern editions, encodings, descriptions, or images.

rights.jurisdiction: United States and jurisdictions where copyright term has expired

entities.description: The early Old French Vulgate Cycle narrative identified by Skeat and later scholarship as the principal source of the Middle English poem. The relation is work-to-work; no specific surviving French manuscript exemplar is asserted.
entities.label: Estoire del saint Graal

entities.description: The distinct codex item that begins in Vernon f. 404vb after Joseph ends in the same folio and column, and continues to f. 406ra. It is included only to make the shared boundary explicit.
entities.label: South English Legendary: Life of Pilate and Judas

next_steps: Search Bodleian administrative and manuscript-department archives for Parker's papers or transcripts.
Search Walter W. Skeat archival holdings and correspondence catalogs.
Retain the intermediary as survival unknown unless an archival record establishes its status.
notes: Skeat's preface establishes that the transcript existed before publication but does not state its later disposition.
openQuestions.question: Does George Parker's working transcript survive, and if so where is it held?

next_steps: Consult David Lawton's 1983 edition and later codicological studies.
Do not convert Skeat's fewer-than-one-hundred-line estimate into a normalized extent without stronger evidence.
openQuestions.question: How much text and which narrative material preceded the defective opening after loss of f. 402?

next_steps: Review the specific contemporary reviews cited by Carley before asserting a more granular metrical model or a Queste lineage edge.
openQuestions.question: How should Lawton's first-draft metrical theory and proposed Queste source contribution be evaluated against the later criticism Carley summarizes?

next_steps: Seek an explicit scope statement from the CME repository maintainer.
Continue treating the local serialization rights as unknown while preserving U-M's current NoC-US item assertion separately.
openQuestions.question: Does the CME repository's Unlicense expressly cover corpus XML text and markup, rather than software alone?

relations.subjectLabel: Bodleian Library MS. Eng. poet. a. 1, ff. 403ra–404vb

reviewNotes: The cataloged subject is the anonymous Middle English poem, not Joseph of Arimathea as a person, the whole Vernon Manuscript, Skeat's whole four-text volume, its appendices, or any digital carrier.

The 709 count records extant Skeat/U-M line positions. It is not a physical manuscript-line count or a reconstruction of the missing opening; Skeat's fewer-than-one-hundred missing lines and approximately 800-line original are historical estimates only.
The witness boundary is asymmetric: the opening is defective after loss of f. 402, while the surviving end appears complete.
Parker's working transcript is modeled as an intermediary because Skeat expressly says Parker made the transcript and Skeat then carefully revised the printed copy.

The complete Bodleian bundle covers all 710 provider canvases. The target occupies only canvases 684–687, and the final canvas is shared: Joseph ends in f. 404vb before the next Pilate/Judas item begins in the same column.
The 34 XML LG elements are encoding containers rather than an assertion of 34 intrinsic stanzas; the Vernon witness presents the poem continuously like prose.

Rights for the medieval verbal work, Skeat's edition, Internet Archive derivatives, Bodleian photography, U-M display, DIMEV data, Carley's scholarship, and local XML serialization are recorded separately.

sourceLinks.entityLabel: George Parker's working transcript of the Vernon Joseph
notes: The negative finding is date-bounded and does not establish that the transcript is lost.

lineage.summary: The repository artifact is the pinned CME XML for University of Michigan item AHA2700.0001.001. Its verbal source is Walter W. Skeat's 1871 edition: the title page and the 709-line poem on printed pp. 1–23, while the XML retains 148 page-break links and placeholders for omitted matter across the full volume. Skeat states that George Parker first prepared a transcript from the sole Vernon Manuscript witness and that Skeat carefully revised that working transcript before printing it, so the graph preserves Parker's transcript between edition and witness. The witness is acephalous because f. 402 is lost, but Skeat judged its ending intact; its exact current locus is ff. 403ra–404vb. A complete 710-canvas Digital Bodleian surrogate is cached, with Joseph mapped to canvases 684–687 and only the Joseph portion of the shared f. 404v end canvas included in the work boundary before the following Life of Pilate and Judas. The medieval work, physical witness, working transcript, edition, scan, OCR derivative, U-M encoding, and checked-in artifact remain distinct, as do their rights layers.

catalogSummary.title: Joseph of Arimathie

catalogingSubject.label: Joseph of Arimathie
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The anonymous Middle English alliterative poem conventionally titled Joseph of Arimathie, surviving acephalously but apparently complete at the end in the Vernon Manuscript, Bodleian Library MS. Eng. poet. a. 1, ff. 403ra–404vb. The subject is the poem represented by 709 extant edited and encoded line positions, not Joseph of Arimathea as a historical or legendary person, the whole Vernon codex, Walter W. Skeat's four-text 1871 volume, its three appended Joseph pieces, the separately bound Legends of the Holy Rood, the University of Michigan encoding and page images, or the checked-in repository XML.

contentParts.label: Surviving acephalous poem

locators.value: ff. 403ra–404vb; f. 402 is missing before the defective opening; the poem ends in column 404vb before the following Life of Pilate and Judas begins on the same folio and column
canvases 684–687 of 710; fols. 403r–404v; the end canvas is shared with the following Pilate/Judas item

notes: The 34 encoded LG containers are retained as representation metrics and are not treated as intrinsic stanzas.
The shared f. 404v/column-b boundary does not include any part of the following South English Legendary Life of Pilate and Judas in Joseph.
contentParts.summary: The 709 extant alliterative long-line positions, beginning mid-sentence with Joseph's release already under discussion and ending intact after the mission of Josaphe and Naciens is announced.

assertion.notes: Carley's modern survey uses c. 1375. Skeat's 1871 linguistic and metrical argument instead preferred about 1350 and no later than 1360; neither approximation is normalized to an exact year.
This dates the codex and attestation, not the poem's composition.

dateStatements.display: Sole surviving witness copied c. 1390–1400, with a contextual lower bound of 1381

scope.description: First printing of the surviving poem

extent.description: One surviving acephalous verse text represented by 709 nonblank BODY L elements. The 709 positions correspond exactly to Skeat's lineation: 172 explicit N attributes mark the expected fourth-line intervals and special positions through line 709, with no positional mismatch. The XML's 34 LG elements vary from 4 to 51 lines and are encoder containers labeled stanza, not a defensible claim that the intellectual work has 34 stanzas. The full encoded carrier has four DIV1 containers—two TYPE='omitted front matter' placeholders, the single BODY TYPE='text' container, and one TYPE='omitted back matter' placeholder—plus a retained TITLEPAGE and 148 PB elements linking page-image scaffolding. Those title-page, placeholder, and PB structures describe the digitized 1871 carrier rather than additional sections or items of the poem, so they are audited here but excluded from numbered_items, prose/verse item counts, verse groups, and verse lines; sections is deliberately null because no intrinsic section count is asserted. Eight manuscript milestones map Skeat's older side-and-column notation from 403a through 404b:2; the current manuscript locus is ff. 403ra–404vb. The witness itself presents the text continuously like prose. The unknown lost opening is not assigned a numeric line count, while the extant endpoint is treated as complete.

formStatements.description: An unrhymed alliterative long-line poem in Skeat's edition and current DIMEV classification. The Vernon scribe wrote it continuously like prose, using capitals, paraphs, and metrical points to signal divisions. Modern scholarship retains the verse classification while debating whether the surviving text is a formally incomplete draft awaiting alliterative revision.

assertion.notes: Carley summarizes Lagorio's and Noble's readings of the adaptation as a hagiographic and didactic saint's life focused on Joseph's evangelizing role.

notes: DIMEV 2.0-alpha.1 records one witness; unique-witness wording is therefore scoped to that exact current census.
All work metadata keeps the medieval work, physical witness, Parker transcript, Skeat edition, U-M encoding, repository artifact, and modern facsimiles as separate layers.
Rights and access for those layers are documented separately in the linked lineage manifest.

next_steps: Consult David Lawton's 1983 critical edition and later codicological studies for any refinement of Skeat's estimate.
Keep the opening extent unknown unless physical or textual evidence supports a number.
notes: Skeat's fewer-than-one-hundred-line loss and approximately 800-line original are historical estimates, not normalized facts.
openQuestions.question: How much text and which exact narrative material preceded the defective opening in the lost f. 402 portion?

next_steps: Consult Lawton 1983 and the reviews summarized by Carley before adding a more granular metrical classification.
Retain verse/alliterative discovery terms while keeping manuscript layout and formal debate explicit.
openQuestions.question: How should the poem's irregular alliterative form and continuous-prose manuscript presentation be described in light of Lawton's first-draft theory and its reviewers?

notes: No exact place of composition is established.

assertion.notes: Skeat argues that the poem was originally West Midland and was southernized by the Vernon scribe; this is a dialect claim, not proof of composition place.
DIMEV links Vernon to LALME/eLALME LP 7630 and Worcestershire. This localizes witness language, not the anonymous poet.

subjects.label: Evalak/Mordrains and the kingdom of Sarras
The White Knight and Evalak's shield

summaries.value: The surviving poem begins after Joseph of Arimathea's forty-two-year imprisonment. Joseph, his son Josaphe, and their company journey to Sarras with Christ's blood; Joseph teaches the Trinity and seeks to convert King Evalak. Evalak's war with Tholomer, the miraculous White Knight, healings, baptisms, and the renaming of Evalak and Seraphe as Mordrains and Naciens occupy the later narrative. The poem ends after Josaphe and Naciens depart on a mission and a brief proleptic account of their imprisonment and rescue.

Only the poem on Skeat's printed pp. 1–23 is cataloged. His prose Lyfe, De sancto Joseph, verse Lyfe, notes, glossaries, indexes, and the separately bound Legends of the Holy Rood remain outside the intellectual-work boundary even though they occur in, or are bibliographically associated with, the same 1871 volume.

The manuscript and current catalogs agree that the opening is defective. Skeat estimated that fewer than one hundred lines were lost and that the original may have had about eight hundred lines, but these are historical estimates, not a recoverable normalized extent. The 709 count describes extant editorial and encoded line positions. Skeat found no loss at the end, so the record preserves the asymmetric boundary: acephalous, apparently complete at end.

The poem's Christian conversion narrative includes polemical representations of Jews and Muslims, mass baptism, punitive violence, and warfare. These features are documented for responsible discovery and interpretation, not endorsed as historical truth.

assertion.notes: The sole manuscript has no surviving opening title; this is a conventional editorial title.

titles.supplied_by: Walter W. Skeat and modern cataloging convention

publication.embeddedTitle: Joseph of Arimathie: otherwise called The romance of the Seint Graal, or Holy Grail: an alliterative poem written about A.D. 1350, and now first printed from the unique copy in the Vernon ms. at Oxford. Edited, with notes and glossarial indices, by Walter W. Skeat.

work.summary: The surviving poem begins after Joseph of Arimathea's forty-two-year imprisonment. Joseph, his son Josaphe, and their company journey to Sarras with Christ's blood; Joseph teaches the Trinity and seeks to convert King Evalak. Evalak's war with Tholomer, the miraculous White Knight, healings, baptisms, and the renaming of Evalak and Seraphe as Mordrains and Naciens occupy the later narrative. The poem ends after Josaphe and Naciens depart on a mission and a brief proleptic account of their imprisonment and rescue.
